coredata.dc:title: Imparting down/up-conversion dual channels fluorescence to luminescence metal-organic frameworks by carbon dots-induced for fluorescence sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Jinli
Zhou, Shu
Tang, Sisi
Wu, Xiaodan
Zhao, Pengfei
Tang, Kangling
Chen, Yu
Yang, Zhaoxia
Zhang, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: The rapid and simple synthesis of highly efficient multi-emission luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) with down/up-conversion fluorescence properties becomes critical in potential optical sensing. Here, we present a luminescent dual-ligand co-assisted strategy to one-pot prepare LMOFs for multiplex fluorescence sensing using carbon dots (CDs) and 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM) as dual-ligand to induce Zn2+ on zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 surface (CDs/MIM-Zn). The CDs/MIM-Zn exhibited efficient fluorescence-enhanced dual channels triple-emission with Stokes and anti-Stokes type excitation profiles. With the assistance of Stokes and anti-Stokes type optical property of CDs and the intrinsic porosity of MOFs structure, a rapid and sensitive dual-channel detection excited at 370 nm and 790 nm was performed to fluorescence turn off-on detect metal ions and reduced glutathione in real blood samples with the satisfactory recoveries of 98.3–106.4%. The dual channels triple-emission strategy ensured multiplex detection of more analytes in parallel.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Coordination-induced
Metal-organic framework
Down/up-conversion dual-channel
Fluorescence-enhanced

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of CDs/MIM-Zn for fluorescence detection
Down/up-conversion dual-channel detection of metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 22164010 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No. 2020JJ4500 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs-Zn, CDs + ZIF-8 and CDs/MIM-Zn excited at 370 nm (A) and 790 nm (B). Fluorescence color of different CDs and synthesized LMOF under UV irradiation at 365 nm (C). Fluorescence emission spectra of various CDs (CDs1, CDs2 and CDs3) and corresponding CDs/MIM-Zn (CDs1/MIM-Zn, CDs2/MIM-Zn and CDs3/MIM-Zn) excited at 370 nm (D) and 790 nm (E). Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs1@ZIF-8 with different CDs1 contents (0.0%, 0.3%, 3.3%, 13.3%, 33.3%, 53.3% and 73.3%) excited at 370 nm (F) and 790 nm (G). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The crystal and fluorescence intensity changes of CDs1/MIM-Zn powders prepared by different CDs1 content under the incandescent lamp and ultraviolet lamp irradiation (A). SEM of CDs1/MIM-Zn containing 3.3% CDs1 (B), 13.3% CDs1 (C) and 33.3% CDs1 (D). TE M images of CDs/MIM-Zn containing 3.3% CDs1 (E), 13.3% CDs1 (F) and 33.3% CDs1 (G). Fluorescence emission spectra of various nanomaterials (2-MIM, CDs1, ZIF-8 and CDs/MIM-Zn) excited at 370 nm (H) and 790 nm (I).

TEM images of CDs/MIM-Zn (A), ZIF-8 (B) and CDs-Zn (C). SEM images of CDs/MIM-Zn (D), ZIF-8 (E) and CDs-Zn (F). HAADF-STEM image and elemental mappings of different elements of C (G), N (H), O (I), and Zn (J) recorded from CDs/MIM-Zn. EDS spectra of CDs/MIM-Zn (K). XRD patterns (L) of ZIF-8 and CDs/MIM-Zn. FT-IR spectra (M) of CDs, ZIF-8 and CDs/MIM-Zn.

Fluorescence intensity of CDs/MIM-Zn excited at 790 nm (A) and 370 nm (B) toward 200 μM of metal ions. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/MIM-Zn at down/up-conversion triple-emission with addition of Fe3+ (C) and Cu2+ (D) concentration ranged from 0.5 μM–400 μM. Corresponding quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) of CDs/MIM-Zn toward Fe3+ and Cu2+ (0.5 μM–400 μM) at emission peak of 460 nm (E) and 530 nm (F) excited at 790 nm as well as emission peak at 450 nm (G) excited at 370 nm.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of CDs/MIM-Zn excited at 370 nm and 790 nm (inset) with addition of Fe3+ (400 μM) and GSH (50 μM). (B) Fluorescence recovery efficiency (F0/F-1) of Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn towards GSH and other disruptors (50 μM). (C) The emission fluorescence spectra of CDs/MIM-Zn before and after addition of 700 μM Fe3+ and GSH (0.5 μM–70 μM) (inset: photos of solution color after irradiation at 365 nm). (D) Corresponding recovery efficiency (F0/F-1) of Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn toward GSH concentration ranged from 0.5 μM to 70 μM (inset: photos of solution under irradiation of visible). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration for fabrication of CDs/MIM-Zn LMOF and fluorescence sensing.

head.ce:title: Imparting down/up-conversion dual channels fluorescence to luminescence metal-organic frameworks by carbon dots-induced for fluorescence sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan, 416000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jishou University, Jishou, 416000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan, 416000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid and simple synthesis of highly efficient multi-emission luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) with down/up-conversion fluorescence properties becomes critical in potential optical sensing. Here, we present a luminescent dual-ligand co-assisted strategy to one-pot prepare LMOFs for multiplex fluorescence sensing using carbon dots (CDs) and 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM) as dual-ligand to induce Zn2+ on zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 surface (CDs/MIM-Zn). The CDs/MIM-Zn exhibited efficient fluorescence-enhanced dual channels triple-emission with Stokes and anti-Stokes type excitation profiles. With the assistance of Stokes and anti-Stokes type optical property of CDs and the intrinsic porosity of MOFs structure, a rapid and sensitive dual-channel detection excited at 370 nm and 790 nm was performed to fluorescence turn off-on detect metal ions and reduced glutathione in real blood samples with the satisfactory recoveries of 98.3–106.4%. The dual channels triple-emission strategy ensured multiplex detection of more analytes in parallel.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) with the functional diversity and structural adjustability, which can better interact with analytes and transform the generated chemical signals into detectable luminescent changes, have been considered as a promising fluorescence sensing material [1,2]. Currently, most reported LMOFs has been constructed by physically encapsulating fluorescence materials into MOF, which resulted in the fluorescence efficiency decreased during separation and purification [3]. Excitingly, the fluorescence emission efficiency of LMOFs can be improved by the coordination-induced of polyamide chains carbon dots (CDs) with hydrogen-bond mediated supramolecular interactions [4–6]. Studies showed introduced CDs with both down/up-conversion fluorescence properties into MOFs is a simpler and rapider strategy for establishing multi-emission LMOFs [7,8].

Multi-emission LMOFs based sensing has attracted considerable attention because of its self-calibration, high sensitivity and short response time [9]. Jalili et al. reported a dual-emission LMOFs for ratiometric fluorescence detection of glutathione with a lower limit of detection (LOD, 0.90 nM) [10]. The ratio signal decreased the background interference via the self-calibration of dual-emissions, improving the accuracy of detection compared with the single signal. Moreover, the amplifying effect from MOFs enhanced the sensitivity in fluorescence sensing [11–14]. It is a fact that the most of the reported LMOFs rely on down-conversion emission, which converted higher energy light into lower energy radiation. This type of sensor suffered serious disadvantages including overlapping of excitation and emission spectra, photo-bleaching and blinking and background auto-fluorescence [9].

Up-conversion nanoparticles with anti-Stokes shift have a unique potential, which converted low energy light into high energy radiation via a non-linear optical process, compensating for the defect of down-conversion fluorescence sensing [15]. However, current up-conversion or down-conversion based ratiometric fluorescence sensors are single-channel detection at one excitation wavelength, easily leading to emission spectrum overlap and signal cross-talk. Kurt et al. developed a dual-excitation sensor based on up-conversion nanoparticle and down-conversion quantum dot, realizing the multiplexed detection for food pathogen [16]. Up to now, no multi-emission LMOFs with down/up-conversion fluorescence based sensor has been reported.

Herein, we firstly reported a luminescent dual-ligand co-assisted strategy to prepare fluorescence enhanced down/up-conversion multi-emission LMOFs for dual channels fluorescence turn off-on detection of metal ions and reduced glutathione (GSH) in blood sample. In this strategy, three typical CDs with Stokes and anti-Stokes type excitation fluorescence properties were prepared separately. Then the CDs and 2-methylimidazole as dual-ligand were coordinated Zn2+ ion to prepare CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs. Notably, the CDs/MIM-Zn was applied in dual channels fluorescence turn off-on detection of metal ions and reduced glutathione (GSH) in blood sample through the complexation mechanism [17].

Citric acids (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), urea, O-phenylenediamine (O-PD), 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Na2SO4, NaNO3, Na3PO4, pyrophosphate (PPi) and chlorinated salts (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+) were purchased from Aladdin Agent Co. (Shanghai, China). GSH, homo-cysteine (Hcy), cysteine (Cys), aspartic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), tryptophan (Trp), valine (Val), arginine (Arg), lysine (Lys) and alanine (Ala) were supplied by Changsha Chemical Reagent Co. (Changsha, China). Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Company (Chengdu, China).

The fluorescence spectra and ultraviolet visible (UV) spectra were performed by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer and Shimadzu UV-1780 spectrophotometer, respectively. The morphology was characterized by FEI TF20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Carl Zeiss AG Gemini 500 scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The crystallinity data recorded in the 2θ range of 10–50° were acquired by a Tongda TD3500 X-ray diffractometer (XRD). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with the Thermoscientific Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. Centrifugal operation was implemented on a Lishen Neofuge 15 high speed centrifuge.

The CDs1 with Stokes and anti-Stokes type excitation profiles were synthesized by the modifying method reported in previous literature [4]. Firstly, 550 μL of EDA was added into 10 mL of deionized water containing 0.5 g of CA. Then, the solution was heated in a Teflon autoclave at 200 °C for 5 h. Cooled to room temperature, the solution was stored directly at 4 °C prior to use without any treatment. Similarly, CDs2 were obtained from 25 mL of water solution containing 0.1 g of urea and 0.2 g of citric under Teflon autoclave heated at 180 °C for 4 h CDs3 were obtained from 50 mL of water solution containing 0.5 g of O-PD under Teflon autoclave heated at 180 °C for 12 h.

2 mL of CDs solution was added into 13 mL of aqueous solution containing 0.25 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O. After magnetic stirred (300 rpm) at room temperature for 30 min, the solid was washed repeatedly with distilled water for 3 times. The CDs-Zn composites were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 5 h.

Briefly, 30 mg of ZIF-8 powder and 2 mL CDs were mixed in a 50 mL round-bottomed flask containing 15 mL aqueous solution by magnetic stirring for 30 min. The CDs + ZIF-8 composites were collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 3 min). Washed with distilled water for 3 times, the CDs + ZIF-8 composites were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 5 h.

CDs/MIM-Zn was prepared by luminescent dual-ligand assisted one-pot strategy. Firstly, 0.25 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 2 mL of CDs solution and 0.5 g of 2-MIM were added into 13 mL of aqueous solution under magnetic stirred (300 rpm) at room temperature for 30 min. Centrifuged (8000 rpm) for 3 min, the CDs/MIM-Zn washed repeatedly with deionized water for 3 times was placed in a vacuum dryer at 60 °C for 5 h.

The fluorescence measurement was implemented on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 1 × 1 cm2 quartz cell. Fluorescence detection with down and up conversion were performed for the same solution. Condition of down conversion: The excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the excitation and emission slit widths of 5.0 nm and photomultiplier tube voltage of 500 V. Condition of up conversion: The excitation wavelength 790 nm, slit 10.0/10.0 and photomultiplier tube voltage of 650 V. The fluorescence detection of analytes was performed with a solution of 1.0 mg/mL CDs/MIM-Zn (pH 6.0) at room temperature.

Real blood sample were acquired from local hospital in accordance with relevant laws of China. According with previous literature [18], blood sample/acetonitrile mixture solution (2/3, v/v) was centrifuged (8000 rpm) for 5 min to remove large molecules and proteins. Then the obtained human serum sample was diluted to 10 times with PBS (pH = 6, 10 mM) for further experiment.

The metal ions detection was based on the down/up-conversion fluorescence turn off mechanism. Various metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+) with a concentration of 200 μM were added into the CDs/MIM-Zn solution (1 mg/mL). Then the triple-emission fluorescence intensities before and after excited at 370 nm and 790 nm were recorded after incubation at room temperature for 2 min. GSH detection was based on the down-conversion fluorescence turn off-on mechanism after the reaction of CDs/MIM-Zn and Fe3+. The GSH solutions with different concentrations range of 0.5–70 μM were added into the Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn (1 mg/mL CDs/MIM-Zn containing 700 μM Fe3+). The fluorescence emission spectra in the range of 400–600 nm at the excitation of 370 nm were recorded after incubation at 25 °C for 2 min.

The synthesis and fluorescence detection process were showed in Scheme 1 . In this work, fluorescence-enhanced CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs with down/up-conversion dual channels triple-emission were constructed. Firstly, down/up-conversion fluorescence polyamide chains CDs with hydrogen-bond mediated supramolecular interactions were synthesized in aqueous solution, with a rigid network structure formed by a compact network of short polyamide chains. Then, the CDs/MIM-Zn was prepared by one-pot polymerization using light-emissive 2-MIM and CDs with down/up-conversion fluorescence properties as dual-ligand to connect Zn2+ ion [18]. Notably, the fluorescence emission of ligands increased gradually during the formation of CDs/MIM-Zn due to the coordination between dual-ligand and Zn2+, which was consistent with the previously reported coordination-induced emission (CIE) [5].

The fluorescence emissions of CDs, CDs-Zn, CDs + ZIF-8 and CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs were shown in Fig. 1 A and B. At the excitation of 370 nm, the CDs/MIM-Zn showed CIE induced blue-shifted (from 475 nm to 450 nm) and fluorescence enhancement compared with CDs. However, the CDs + ZIF-8 obtained by physical adsorption CDs showed a weak emission peak consistent with CD at 475 nm. Excitingly, compared with the emission of CDs ligand at 475 nm, the obtained luminescent CDs-Zn prepared by CDs fluorescence ligand bonding Zn2+ showed the fluorescence-enhanced down/up-conversion dual channel dual-emission and blue-shifted (from 475 nm to 450 nm) due to the CIE, with similar as the performance of CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs. On the other hand, at excitation of 790 nm, the CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs showed blue-shift (480 nm–460 nm) compared with CDs. CDs + ZIF-8 showed the similar results as down-conversion channel excited at 370 nm without the blue-shifted, which indicated that physical adsorption CDs does not cause CIE. Interestingly, the CDs-Zn exhibited the fluorescence-enhanced and blue-shifted compared with the pure CDs, showing the strong coordination of CDs with Zn2+. The above data proved that CDs participates in the synthesis of CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs through chemical coordination with Zn2+ rather than the physical adsorption of CD to ZIF-8, leading to fluorescence enhancement and blue-shifted caused by CIE [3]. In addition, In order to synthesize CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs with best fluorescence performance, the synthesis conditions were investigated.

The CDs type is the key to the synthesis of CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs with fluorescence-enhanced down/up-conversion dual channels triple-emission. Three kinds of CDs/MIM-Zn were synthesized based on previous reported CDs with the some modifications [4,10]. As shown in Fig. 1C, pure CDs and the corresponding synthetic MOFs in powder and aqueous solution showed the obvious fluorescence color under UV irradiated at 365 nm. The obtained CDs1/MIM-Zn and CDs2/MIM-Zn have the same blue fluorescence color as CDs1 and CDs2 (Fig. 1C1 and Fig. 1C2). CDs3/MIM-Zn exhibited the fluorescence color change from yellow to green compared with the CDs3 (Fig. 1C3). As shown in Fig. 1D, when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, CDs1/MIM-Zn, CDs2/MIM-Zn and CDs3/MIM-Zn all showed the fluorescence-enhanced and significant blue-shifted compared with the corresponding CDs, which was caused by the rigid CDs being distorted to adapt to the rigid structure of MOFs [5]. However, when the excitation wavelength was 790 nm (Fig. 1E), only the CDs1/MIM-Zn LMOF exhibited the significant fluorescence-enhanced up-conversion dual-emission at 460 nm and 530 nm, which originated from the luminescence of dual-ligand of CDs and 2-MIM (Figure S1). The above results showed the feasibility to prepare CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs with fluorescence-enhanced down/up-conversion dual channels performance. Thus, the CDs1/MIM-Zn LMOFs with significant fluorescence-enhanced dual channels triple-emission were selected for subsequent analysis.

The amount of CDs1 will affect the fluorescence performance of CDs1/MIM-Zn LMOFs. The fluorescence-enhanced and blue-shifted emission performances of CDs1/MIM-Zn prepared from CDs1 with different ratios in aqueous solution (0.0%, 0.3%, 3.3%, 13.3%, 33.3%, 53.3% and 73.3%) were discussed in detail. As shown in Fig. 1F, when the CDs1/MIM-Zn was excited at 370 nm, the down-conversion fluorescence intensity of the CDs1/MIM-Zn increased with the amount of CDs1 increased to 33.3%, then decreased after the amount of CDs1 increased over 33.3%, which was due to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [19]. In addition, the blue-shifted (from 450 nm to 445 nm) caused by CIE can also be found in the fluorescence emission spectra. The results were consistent with the previous report [5]. Moreover, the fluorescence enhancement excited at 790 nm showed in Fig. 1G increased gradually with the increase of the amount of CDs1, and decreased after reaching the threshold (33.3% CDs1) because of the ACQ. In addition, the emission peak from CDs1 showed weak red-shifted (from 458 nm to 462 nm) due to the coordination. The above results indicated that the CDs1/MIM-Zn prepared by 13.3% and 33.3% CDs1 has good down-conversion emission at 450 nm and up-conversion dual-emission fluorescence peaks (460 nm and 530 nm). In order to obtain a wider response range for the detection of analyte, the fluorescence response of CDs1/MIM-Zn prepared from 13.3% CDs1 and 33.3% CDs1 toward Fe3+ was analyzed (Figure S2). The results showed that CDs1/MIM-Zn prepared from 13.3% CDs exhibited a wider response range in the presence of both peaks (460 nm and 530 nm). Thus 13.3% CDs was selected to prepare the optimized CDs1/MIM-Zn.

Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs1/MIM-Zn prepared by different volumes CDs1 were observed by the naked eye. As shown in Fig. 2 A–a, under the incandescent lamp, the color of the synthesized CDs1/MIM-Zn were significantly deepened. In addition, the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs1/MIM-Zn powders under UV irradiation at 365 nm was similar with the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1F). In addition, the morphology of CDs1/MIM-Zn prepared with different CDs1 contents (3.3%, 13.3% and 33.3%) was characterized by SEM and TEM in detail. As shown in Fig. 2B, the aggregations of CDs1/MIM-Zn nanosheets were observed in SEM of CDs1/MIM-Zn prepared by 3.3% CDs1. When the CDs1 content increased to 13.3%, the aggregative nanosheets changed to nanospheres (Fig. 2C). However, when the content of CDs1 increased to 33.3%, a rough coating layer appeared on the surface (Fig. 2D). The TEM of the CDs1/MIM-Zn containing 3.3% CDs1 exhibited the crossed aggregation with the size of 200 nm (Fig. 2E). Then, the CDs1/MIM-Zn containing 13.3% CDs1 showed core-shell structure with the size of 350 nm, and the twisted aggregates with size of 180 nm can be seen (Fig. 2F). Finally, when the content of CDs1 increased to 33.3%, the size of the CDs1/MIM-Zn sphere increased to about 400 nm, in which spherical particles with the core-shell structure can be observed (Fig. 2G). The results showed that the CDs1/MIM-Zn exhibited different MOFs structure by tuning the ratio of CDs and water. The CDs1/MIM-Zn with core-shell structure exhibited strong emission because more fluorescence groups from CDs were exposed on the MOFs surface [5].

Since most of reports on the synthesis of CDs1/MIM-Zn LMOF were used methanol as solvent, the fluorescence properties of CDs1/MIM-Zn synthesized with water and methanol as solvent were discussed. The CDs1/MIM-Zn prepared with different volumes of CDs1 (3.3%, 13.3%, 33.3%, 53.3%) in methanol or water was discussed. As shown in Figure S3 and S4, when methanol was used as solvent, great effect on the up-conversion fluorescence intensity of CDs1/MIM-Zn was observed at 530 nm with no change on the down-conversion fluorescence intensity. In contrast, the CDs1/MIM-Zn synthesized with water as solvent has better performance.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs at two excitation wavelengths (370 nm and 790 nm) were analyzed in detail. As shown in Fig. 2H and I, the CDs/MIM-Zn exhibited down/up-conversion dual channels triple-emission excited at 370 nm and 790 nm due to the coordination of fluorescence dual-ligand (2-MIM and CDs) and metal ion (Zn2+). In addition, the fluorescence ligand of CDs showed down/up-conversion dual channels fluorescence emission (475 nm and 470 nm) excited at 370 nm and 790 nm. Based on the previous reports [1,4], it was assumed that the down-conversion channel fluorescence originated from well-known CO and the up-conversion fluorescence originated from two-photon induced photochemical response caused by N-containing heterocyclic ring ligands [20–23]. At the excitation of 370 nm, the CDs/MIM-Zn showed CIE induced blue-shifted (from 475 nm to 450 nm) and fluorescence enhancement, which was attributed to the plane structure of ligand CDs was twisted to adapt to the MOFs rigid structure. In addition, ZIF-8 showed the weak emission at 430 nm, hardly affecting the down-conversion emission of CDs/MIM-Zn. At the excitation of 790 nm, the CDs/MIM-Zn showed the dual-emissions at 460 nm and 530 nm were enhanced with blue-shift (from 480 nm to 460 nm) compared with pure CDs and ZIF-8. Thus, the CDs1/MIM-Zn synthesized by the dual-ligand co-assisted strategy, exhibited fluorescence enhanced down/up-conversion dual channels triple-emission induced by CIE.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, ZIF-8 and CDs/MIM-Zn were shown in Figure S5. The absorption peak of CDs1 at 240 nm was ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC bond of aromatic sp2 carbons, while the weak absorption peak at 350 nm was ascribed to the n-π* transitions of CO/CN functional groups [4]. In addition, the CDs1 has the same absorption peak as ZIF-8 at 215 nm due to the π-π* transition of N-containing aromatic C–C bonds [4,6]. Interestingly, the CDs/MIM-Zn prepared by dual-ligand (CDs and 2-MIM) connecting Zn2+ ion only showed the absorption peak at 215 nm, while the absorption peaks at 240 nm and 350 nm belonging to CDs1 disappeared due to the coordination. Thus, the above analysis demonstrated the successful connection of the dual-ligand (CDs and 2-MIM) with Zn2+ to prepare the CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs.

The TEM images of CDs/MIM-Zn LMOF (Fig. 3 A) showed the core-shell nanospheres with average size of 240 nm. Compared with the pure ZIF-8, an accumulational and distorted structure with sized of 120 nm hexagonal prism was existed in the interior of the CDs/MIM-Zn (Fig. 3B). The outer shell is probably CDs-Zn formed by the coordination of CDs with Zn2+, and the TEM characterization result was shown in Fig. 3C. The significant differences originated from the introduced of CDs as ligand, which coordinated with 2-MIM during the preparation of LMOF. Moreover, the SEM of CDs/MIM-Zn and pure ZIF-8 shown in Fig. 3D and E indicated tiny nanoparticles attached to the outer surface of CDs/MIM-Zn, which was attributed to the coordination of ligand CDs and Zn2+ resulting in a three-dimensional mesh of CDs-Zn (SEM in Fig. 3F). Moreover, the HAADF-STEM image and element mappings of CDs/MIM-Zn showed that C, N, O, and Zn were equably distributed in the nanostructure (Fig. 3G–J), further confirming the analysis of EDS spectra (Fig. 3K).

The XRD patterns of crystal structures of ZIF-8, CDs/MIM-Zn were shown in Fig. 3L. The ZIF-8 showed high crystallinity and sharp diffraction peaks, in which typical characteristic diffraction peaks positions (from 10 to 30) matched the rhomboid dodecahedron crystal structure [24]. However, the CDs/MIM-Zn not only showed the same diffraction peak as ZIF-8, but also showed many small-size diffraction peaks due to the participation of CDs ligands, which impacted the crystalline integrity of ZIF-8. The results demonstrated the successful preparation of CDs/MIM-Zn.

The FT-IR spectra of CDs, ZIF-8 and CDs/MIM-Zn were shown in Fig. 3M. The absorption peaks located at 1560 cm−1 in the spectrum of CDs was attributed to the stretching vibration of amide (N–CO) synthesized from CA and EDA. Moreover, the stretching vibration of CC (1400 cm−1), C–O (1140 cm−1), C–N (1300 cm−1), and N–H/O–H (3200-3500 cm−1) was observed, which suggested that the CDs are mainly composed of polyaromatic structures with functional oxygen and nitrogen groups, contributing to the rigidity of the system [20]. After coordination of dual-ligand (CDs and 2-MIM) with Zn2+ to form CDs/MIM-Zn, the peaks presented at 3144 cm−1, 2918 cm−1 and 3200-3500 cm−1 in the spectra of CDs/MIM-Zn were attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H and N–H/O–H in aromatic and aliphatic group. Notably, stretching vibration of CO at 1665 cm−1 and N–CO at 1560 cm−1 was mainly contributed from CDs and peaks centered at 1415 cm−1 and 990 cm−1 belonged to the tensile vibration and plane bending vibration of imidazole rings. In addition, CDs/MIM-Zn also showed the Zn–O (750 cm−1) and Zn–N (422 cm−1) absorption peak, which was similar with ZIF-8. Thus, the above data indicated the synthesis of CDs/MIM-Zn LMOF by the dual-ligand co-assisted strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CDs/MIM-Zn for fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: The factors affecting the fluorescence sensing of CDs/MIM-Zn such as excitation wavelength and solution pH value were discussed. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/MIM-Zn at different excitation wavelengths were shown in Figure S6 and S7. The results showed that the down-conversion fluorescence emission peak was independent of excitation wavelength. At 370 nm excitation, CDs/MIM-Zn has a maximum emission wavelength of 450 nm. On the other hand, the up-conversion fluorescence emission wavelength was related to the excitation wavelength. At 790 nm excitation, CDs/MIM-Zn showed excellent dual-emission (460 nm and 530 nm). In addition, red-shifted was occurred with the increase of excitation wavelength, showing the wavelength-dependent behavior [6]. Thus, the excitation wavelength of 370 nm and 790 nm were selected as the best excitation wavelength for the CDs/MIM-Zn with down/up-conversion fluorescence performance.

The fluorescence intensities of CDs/MIM-Zn in aqueous solutions with different pH values ranged from 4.0 to 11.0 were studied. As shown in Figure S8 and S9, the acidic solution (pH, 4.0–6.0) has a great influence on the fluorescence intensity under both excitation wavelength at 790 nm and 370 nm. At pH 6.0, the down/up-conversion fluorescence intensity of CDs/MIM-Zn is the best. Therefore, pH 6.0 was selected for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Down/up-conversion dual-channel detection of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The down/up-conversion dual channels triple-emission fluorescence intensity of the CDs/MIM-Zn before and after addition of different metal ions (200 μM) was analyzed. The fluorescence detection of chlorinated salts (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ag + Fe2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+) were performed at room temperature with a solution of 1.0 mg/mL CDs/MIM-Zn (pH 6.0). As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, at the excitation of 790 nm, the CDs/MIM-Zn showed the strong up-conversion fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ and Cu2+ among various metal ions, which was ascribed to Fe3+ and Cu2+ stronger affinity with fluorescence group of CDs/MIM-Zn [3]. However, only obvious down-conversion fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ was observed under excitation at 370 nm. In addition, the blue fluorescence became dim compared to other metal ions under UV irradiation at 365 nm (inset a in Fig. 4B), which was attributed to the binding energy of chelates formed by Fe3+ was greater than chelates formed by Cu2+ [25]. The results showed that the CDs/MIM-Zn was a triple-response, sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for the detection Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions over other common metal ions. In addition, the response time of CDs/MIM-Zn detecting Fe3+ and Cu2+ was studied (Figure S10 and S11). The fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/Fn-1) at different emission peaks was investigated at different response times. After addition of 200 μM Fe3+ and Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity at dual channels triple-emission peaks (450, 460 and 530 nm) of the CDs/MIM-Zn all decreased and then rapidly achieved stability within 2.0 min. In addition, the chelate can be observed after the addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with the naked eye (inset b in Fig. 4B). The shorter response time of the CDs/MIM-Zn may be attributed to the large surface area of ZIF-8 [9].

The detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ was performed by comparing the fluorescence intensity of down/up-conversion dual channels triple-emission peaks excited at 370 and 790 nm after the addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ into CDs/MIM-Zn solution. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, for the up-conversion channel excited at 790 nm, the fluorescence intensities emitted at 460 nm and 530 nm were quenched obviously after the addition of various concentrations (0.5–400 μM) of Fe3+ and Cu2+ into CDs/MIM-Zn solution. However, at the down-conversion channel excited at 370 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MIM-Zn was affected greatly by Fe3+ but only slightly changes by Cu2+. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) of CDs/MIM-Zn by Fe3+ and Cu2+ at emission of 460 nm and 530 nm under excited at 790 nm were shown in Fig. 4E and F. Excellent good linear calibration curve was existed between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions concentration range of 0.5–400 μM with the detection limit of (LOD, 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the lowest signal (n = 9) and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot) 20 nM (Fe3+) and 30 nM (Cu2+). The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) of CDs/MIM-Zn toward Fe3+ and Cu2+ at emission of 450 nm under excited at 370 nm were shown in Fig. 4G, showing excellent good linear response in the range of 0.5–400 μM. The above results indicated that the sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ can be realized by CDs/MIM-Zn with down/up-conversion triple-emission fluorescence. In addition, the performance comparison of different fluorescence sensing probe for detecting Fe3+ were showed in Table S1. Since most of the previous fluorescence sensing platforms relied on conventional down-conversion fluorescence, the CDs/MIM-Zn developed in this study exhibited the superiority of down/up-conversion triple-response for ultra-sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a wider linear range and lower LOD.

The effect of GSH concentration on the down/up-conversion dual channels fluorescence intensity of pure CDs/MIM-Zn was firstly evaluated and the results were shown in Figure S12 and S13. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged after the addition of 0.5–70 μM GSH into CDs/MIM-Zn solution, confirming GSH has no interaction with the pure CDs/MIM-Zn. In addition, it is feasible to detect GSH with fluorescence sensor quenched by metal ions in the previous literature.10 The fluorescence intensity of CDs/MIM-Zn excited at 790 nm and 370 nm was recorded after addition of Fe3+ (400 μM) and GSH (50 μM). As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence of CDs/MIM-Zn with the down/up-conversion channel excited at 790 nm and 370 nm was quenched by Fe3+. After addition of GSH, the fluorescence of the CDs/MIM-Zn with the down-conversion channel excited at 370 nm was restored, but the fluorescence of the CDs/MIM-Zn with the up-conversion channel excited at 790 nm was not changed.

The response times under down-conversion channel excited at 370 nm of the Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn toward GSH was studied. As shown in Figure S14, the fluorescence intensity recovered rapidly and then achieved stability within 2.0 min after addition of 50 μM GSH. The anti-interference of the Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn toward GSH was investigated in presence of other amino acids and disruptors (50 μM), such as Ala, Lys, Arg, Val, Asp, Trp, Gly, Cys, Hcy, Glu, AA, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, PO4 3− and PPi. As shown in Fig. 5B, the tested inorganic salts and amino acids showed little interference, except Cys, Hcy and PPi have a certain intervention on the selectivity of Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn toward GSH. The interference of Cys and Hcy was caused that they contained sulfhydryl group chelated strongly with Fe3+ like GSH [26]. Although Cys and Hcy have similar chemical structures with GSH, they exhibited fluorescence recovery capability in the order of GSH > Hcy > Cys, which can be explained by decreased steric hindrance effect in the order of GSH > Hcy > Cys due to the involvement of simple molecular structure [27]. However, the intervention of Cys, Hcy and PPi cannot affect the detection of Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn toward GSH in human blood since the concentration of GSH was much higher than that of Cys, Hcy and PPi [28].

The detection of GSH by Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn based on fluorescence turn off-on of down-conversion channel at excitation of 370 nm was studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 5C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MIM-Zn decreased rapidly after the addition of Fe3+ (700 μM), and stabilized after 2 min. Then, the fluorescence intensity of above solution recovered positively upon the GSH concentration (0.5–70 μM) and the fluorescence intensity can be recovered up to 80% in comparison with the pristine CDs/MIM-Zn. The photo images in the inset of Fig. 5C showed the change in fluorescence intensity before and after addition of GSH, indicating the turn off-on characteristic for down-conversion channel detection of GSH using Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn. In addition, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0-1) showed a good linear relationship (y = 0.0318 C + 0.0031, R2 = 0.995) with the change of GSH concentration (Fig. 5D), which showed an excellent linear and wider calibration range of 0.5–70 μM with a lower LOD of 15 nM. Significantly, In the process of detecting GSH with Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn, the appearance of crystals was visible by the naked eye (inset of Fig. 5D) due to the strong chelation. Thus, a down/up-conversion dual channel detection system for the fluorescence turn off-on detection of GSH was developed through the complexation mechanism between GSH and Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn. Table S2 showed the performance comparison of the detection of GSH with different fluorescence sensors. Compared with the traditional single down-conversion detection, the down/up-conversion dual-channel sensor achieved the parallel detection toward GSH, which showed the self-calibration.

In this work, the fluorescence of the CDs/MIM-Zn was firstly quenched by Fe3+ ions, and then fluorescence was restored by GSH. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs/MIM-Zn by Fe3+ ion was ascribed to the charge-transfer process between Fe3+ and surface functional groups of CDs/MIM-Zn. The charge-transfer process has been demonstrated by insignificant change of average fluorescence lifetime in previous reports [17]. Once Fe3+ ion was attached to the surface of CDs/MIM-Zn via –COO–Fe3+-OH interaction, the charge-transfer hindered the emission traps of CDs/MIM-Zn, leading to the quench of down-conversion fluorescence intensity [4]. On the other hand, the amides and N-containing aromatic rings showed up-conversion fluorescence also can be strongly chelated with Fe3+, causing the favorable non-radiative relaxant pathway and possibly reducing the up-conversion channel fluorescence emission [5,9]. The mechanism of fluorescence recovers depends on GSH/Fe3+ complexation reaction, where sulfhydryl group of GSH plays an important role. In presence of GSH, diminished down-conversion luminescence can be restored. Fe3+ forms a stable complex with GSH through acidic –COOH and basic –NH2 groups. Due to GSH/Fe3+ complexation, the trap on CDs/MIM-Zn was updated, and the down-conversion channel fluorescence intensity increased with the increase of GSH concentration [10]. However, the up-conversion channel fluorescence was no recovery, possibly because GSH could not offer the free chemical groups with up-conversion fluorescence from the Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn [6].

To evaluate the real sample analysis by CDs/MIM-Zn, determination of Fe3+ ion and GSH was performed in diluted human serum. Clinical verification showed that the original amount of GSH in diluted human serum was 2.178 μM, and the normal concentration of Fe3+ was 30 μM [27,29]. Then, spiking recovery studies were carried out with the addition of definite amounts of Fe3+ and GSH in serum sample, and verifying the results by inductively coupled plasma (ICP) and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), respectively. The spiking detection of Fe3+ was performed by the CDs/MIM-Zn, while GSH was detected by the Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn after quenching by Fe3+ (700 μM). Table S3 and Table S4 showed the satisfying recoveries of Fe3+ and GSH ranged from 98.3% to 106.4%, corresponding to the results of instrument method. The overall results indicated the fluorescence sensor of CDs/MIM-Zn was a promising sensing platform for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ and GSH in clinical real samples.

In summary, a dual-ligand co-assisted strategy was developed to rapidly and simply prepare fluorescence-enhanced triple-emission CDs/MIM-Zn LMOFs with Stokes and anti-Stokes type excitation profiles for dual channels fluorescence turn off-on detection of metal ions and GSH. The CDs/MIM-Zn showed fluorescence-enhanced emission from dual-ligand of CDs and 2-MIM and blue-shifted due to the one pot coordination. The CDs/MIM-Zn with triple-emission and stronger fluorescence emission as well as CIE induced blue-shifted realized the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with the detection limits of 20 nM and 30 nM, respectively. Then, the Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn was used to detect GSH in the concentration ranges of 0.5–70 μM with the detection limit of 15 nM. The fluorescence sensor with excellent anti-interference was successfully applied to the analysis of real blood samples with satisfactory recoveries of 98.3–105.2%.

Jinli Fu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shu Zhou: Methodology. Sisi Tang: Data curation. Xiaodan Wu: Supervision. Pengfei Zhao: Validation. Kangling Tang: Formal analysis. Yu Chen: Resources. Zhaoxia Yang: Supervision, Project administration. Zhaohui Zhang: Funding acquisition, Writing-Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 22164010), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No.2020JJ4500).

ce:section.ce:para:
